content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662                322-5660              322-5901              322-6441              322-6444


October 4, 1982


John F. Powell                                                EXPRESS MAIL
District Counsel
Bay Area Air Quality
Management District
939 Ellis Street
San Francisco, Ca  94109

                                                    Re: Your Letter to Chairperson
                                                        Helen Rudee, Dated
                                                        September 9, 1982

Dear Mr. Powell:

     You have brought the above-referenced letter to the
Commission's attention. This letter was written by you to
advise members of the Bay Area Air Quality Management District
of their duties relating to conflicts of interest. We are
concerned about some of the advice rendered in your letter.

     On page 7 of your letter you state that the effect (of a
governmental decision) must be on the "pocketbook" of the
official in order for a conflict of interest to exist. On page
8 of your letter you correctly state that the Commission
interprets Government Code Section 87103(a) as stating that the
effect in question need only be on the business entity in which
the official holds an investment in order for a conflict of
interest to exist. Your letter goes on to imply that the
Commission's interpretation is not consistent with the Political
Reform Act ("Act").1/

     Under the Political Reform Act, the Commission is the agency
vested with responsibility for interpreting the Act. The
Commission's interpretation of the disqualification requirements


_________________________

     1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise
specified.

John F. Powell
October 4, 1982
Page 2


of the Act is entirely consistent with the language of the Act.
Section 87103 defines the meaning of a financial interest as
follows:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on:

     (a) Any business entity in which the official has a
direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand
dollars ($1,000).

     (b) Any real property in which....

     (c) Any source of income....

     (d) Any business entity in which the public official
is director, officer, partner, trustee, employee or holds
any position of management.

               (Emphasis added.)

It is unquestionable that the issue in Section 87103 is the
effect of the decision on the business entity, not on the
official's investment in the business entity. Adding emphasis
to this point is the fact that the same language is used in
subdivision (a) as in subdivision (d), even though subdivision
(d) requires no investment at all. The language of Section
87103 is unambiguous on that point.

     This interpretation has been fully supported by the courts
and is incorporated in the Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm.
Code section 18702. Your letter fails to consider the decision
in Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817; 139 Cal. Rptr.
161, hrg. den. Aug. 11, 1977. In that case, the defendant
Floyd L. Morrow argued unsuccessfully that the effect of the
decision must be on his pocketbook, not merely on the business
entity (BEE) which was his employer. There the Court stated:

     Morrow claims decisions concerning the redevelopment in
Linda Vista have no "material financial effect" on him
because he receives a set amount each month. The unstated
implication is even if BEE were to reap large profits
Morrow's salary would remain the same. However, the statute

John F. Powell
October 4, 1982
Page 3


     is concerned with whether there will be "a material
financial effect ... on [a]ny source of income [over
$250]....." (Italics ours - Section 87103.)

* * *

     Morrow asserts it is unconstitutional to automatically
disqualify a public official from participating in decisions
which may affect the investments of an entity which pays him
over $250 per year. However, the whole purpose of the
Political Reform Act of 1974 is to preclude a government
official from participating in decisions where it appears he
may not be totally objective because the outcome will likely
benefit a corporation or individual by whom he is also
employed.  [Cite.]  That is the situation here.

     Witt v. Morrow, supra at 822-23.

     Your clients should be on notice that the Commission will
enforce the provisions of the Act in accordance with its
announced interpretation of the Act, as set forth in its
regulations and opinions.  Because we have specifically informed
you that your advice has been in error, we will not consider
reliance by any of your clients upon the advice contained in
your letter as evidence of "good faith," "inadvertence" or
"negligence" within the meaning of Section 91001(c), in the
event that any of them violates Sections 87100 and 87103.

     As you agreed we are sending a copy of our pamphlet directly
to your board members, together with a cover letter emphasizing
our position.  We have also reminded them that they may seek our
advice, which if followed may provide immunity from penalties
for any possible violations of the Act.  At your request, a copy
of the pamphlet and letter is enclosed.  We are enclosing the
letters and pamphlets for the board members with this mailing
and ask that they be delivered to the board members prior to the
meeting on Wednesday, October 6th.  You have stated that you
will furnish us with copies of your materials to them on this
matter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Ted Prim
                                        Executive Director

TP:REL:plh
Enclosures:  A Guide to the Political Reform Act of 1974
             Form Letter to Board Members, BAAQMD

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660           322-5901         322-6441              592-6444

October 4, 1982


Board Member
Bay Area Air Quality
Management District
939 Ellis Street
San Francisco, CA  94109

Dear *** NOT ENOUGH MERGE ITEMS ***:

Our records indicate that you are a member of the Bay
Area Air Quality Management District (BAAQMD).  Questions
have come to our attention regarding possible conflicts of
interest involving various decisions made by the BAAQMD
board, specifically decisions which may affect certain
business entities.

Because of our responsibility to assure that the
provisions of the Political Reform Act are complied with by
all public officials, we are taking this opportunity to
send to you a copy of our explanatory pamphlet "A Guide to
the Political Reform Act of 1974 - California's Conflict of
Interest Law for Public Officials."

The pamphlet explains what constitutes a financial
interest and how decisions which may affect those interests
can give rise to conflicts of interest requiring
disqualification.  You should note that in order for you to
have a conflict of interest, the effect of a decision need
only be on your employer, your real property or any company
in which you have an investment.  The decision need not
directly affect the official's own finances.

The conflict of interest provisions contained in the
Political Reform Act define the meaning of a financial
interest as follows:

An official has a financial interest in a decision
...if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on:

*** NOT ENOUGH MERGE ITEMS ***
October 4, 1982
Page 2


     (a) Any business entity in which the official has
a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

     (b) Any real property in which....

     (c) Any source of income....

     (d) Any business entity in which the public
official is director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee or holds any position of management.

                            Government Code Section 87103.
                            (Emphasis added.)

     The issue in Section 87103 is the effect of the
decision on the business entity, not on the official's
investment in the business entity. The language of Section
87103 is unambiguous on that point.

     This reading of the Political Reform Act has been fully
supported by the courts and is incorporated in the
Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.
See, Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal.App.3d 817; 139 Cal.
Rptr. 161, hrg. den. Aug. 11, 1977. In that case, the
defendant Floyd L. Morrow argued unsuccessfully, that he
was not guilty of a conflict of interest because the
effects of his decisions were not on his pocketbook, but
merely on the business entity which was his employer.

     As also explained in the pamphlet, a violation of the
disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act can
lead to administrative, civil and criminal penalties.
However, reliance upon written advice by the Fair Political
Practices Commission provides certain immunities to
officials who have requested that advice. Should you have
any questions regarding your duties under the Act, please
do not hesitate to contact the Commission staff. The Legal
Division's phone number is (916) 322-5901.

                            Sincerely,


                            Ted Prim
                            Executive Director

TP:plh
Enclosure

Edward Bacciocco

Joseph Bort

Barbara Boxer

Harry Britt

Sam Chapman

Fred Cooper

Thomas Corcoran

John Cunningham

Emanuele Damonte

Rod Diridon

Ralph Doetzsch

Milton Feldstein

Laython Landis

Dan McCorquodale

Sunne Wright McPeak

Frank Ogawa

Helen Rudee

Carol Silver

Roberta Hughan

sections.conclusion: The Commission states that Section 87103 unambiguously requires evaluation of the effect on the business entity itself, not on the official's personal finances. The language is consistent with Witt v. Morrow (1977) and the Commission's regulations. The District Counsel's advice was erroneous, and reliance upon it will not constitute good faith, inadvertence, or negligence if violations occur.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a District Counsel's letter correctly advised board members that a financial conflict of interest requires a material effect on an official's personal 'pocketbook' rather than on a business entity in which the official has an investment or employment interest.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC rejects the District Counsel's interpretation and clarifies that conflicts of interest exist when a decision has material financial effect on a business entity in which an official has an investment or employment relationship, regardless of whether the official's personal pocketbook is affected. The FPPC will enforce the Political Reform Act according to its announced interpretation and will not credit reliance on the erroneous advice as a defense.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is partially degraded with OCR errors in second and third pages, but the main letter (pages 1-3) is clearly readable. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present, but the document is clearly an FPPC response letter correcting erroneous advice. Document ID 82A167 indicates this is an archived advice letter from 1982.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a District Counsel's letter correctly advised board members that a financial conflict of interest requires a material effect on an official's personal 'pocketbook' rather than on a business entity in which the official has an investment or employment interest.

CONCLUSION: The Commission states that Section 87103 unambiguously requires evaluation of the effect on the business entity itself, not on the official's personal finances. The language is consistent with Witt v. Morrow (1977) and the Commission's regulations. The District Counsel's advice was erroneous, and reliance upon it will not constitute good faith, inadvertence, or negligence if violations occur.

embedding.summary: The FPPC corrects erroneous legal advice given by a District Counsel to board members of the Bay Area Air Quality Management District regarding conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC clarifies that a financial interest exists when a decision affects a business entity in which an official has an investment or employment relationship, not merely when it affects the official's personal finances.
